verses.text: The prophet cries, 'O LORD, how long shall I cry, and thou wilt not hear!' Justice is perverted and the wicked surround the righteous. An honest lament. Faith may pour out its perplexity to God.
The prophet cries, 'O LORD, how long shall I cry, and thou wilt not hear!' Justice is perverted and the wicked surround the righteous. An honest lament. Faith may pour out its perplexity to God.
The prophet cries, 'O LORD, how long shall I cry, and thou wilt not hear!' Justice is perverted and the wicked surround the righteous. An honest lament. Faith may pour out its perplexity to God.
The prophet cries, 'O LORD, how long shall I cry, and thou wilt not hear!' Justice is perverted and the wicked surround the righteous. An honest lament. Faith may pour out its perplexity to God.
God answers: behold and wonder, for He will raise up the Chaldeans, 'that bitter and hasty nation,' to do a work none would believe. A startling reply. God works by means we would never expect.
God answers: behold and wonder, for He will raise up the Chaldeans, 'that bitter and hasty nation,' to do a work none would believe. A startling reply. God works by means we would never expect.
They are terrible and dreadful, their horses swifter than leopards, fiercer than wolves, flying like the eagle. Fearsome foe. God's chosen rod is a mighty and merciless army.
They are terrible and dreadful, their horses swifter than leopards, fiercer than wolves, flying like the eagle. Fearsome foe. God's chosen rod is a mighty and merciless army.
They come for violence, sweeping up captives like sand, scoffing at kings, and crediting their power to their god. Proud conquerors. Yet they 'impute' to an idol the strength God gave them.
They come for violence, sweeping up captives like sand, scoffing at kings, and crediting their power to their god. Proud conquerors. Yet they 'impute' to an idol the strength God gave them.

'Art thou not from everlasting?' the prophet pleads; Thou art 'of purer eyes than to behold evil'; why look on the treacherous? A deeper puzzle. How can a holy God use the wicked to punish the less wicked?

'Art thou not from everlasting?' the prophet pleads; Thou art 'of purer eyes than to behold evil'; why look on the treacherous? A deeper puzzle. How can a holy God use the wicked to punish the less wicked?
Men are made like fishes, caught in the foe's net, to which he sacrifices; shall he keep emptying his net and not spare? A bold question. The prophet lays his hardest doubt before God.
